title: Two Pointers
description: Master the two-pointer technique to slash O(n²) brute-force loops into elegant O(n) solutions across sorted arrays, subarrays, and string problems on Codeforces.

sections.content: 🎯 The Competitive Programming Mindset

Two pointers is one of the first techniques that separates Codeforces 1200 solvers from 1400+ solvers. Whenever you see a problem involving a **sorted array**, a **subarray condition**, or **two sequences** that need to be merged or compared, your brain should immediately ask: *can two pointers reduce the inner loop to O(1) amortized?* The pointer never moves backwards in most variants — making the total traversal O(n) regardless of the outer loop.

sections.label: When to Reach for Two Pointers
sections.content: Pattern triggers: `find pair with sum X`, `longest subarray satisfying condition`, `count pairs`, `merge two sorted arrays`, `remove duplicates in-place`, `palindrome check`. If the array is **sorted** or you're dealing with a **sliding window**, two pointers almost certainly applies.
📐 Pattern 1 — Opposite Ends (Left + Right)

Place `lo` at index 0 and `hi` at index `n-1`. Move them toward each other based on a comparison. Works perfectly on **sorted arrays** when looking for pairs with a target sum, or checking if a string is a palindrome.

sections.title: Two Sum on Sorted Array — O(n)

sections.label: Why This is O(n)
sections.content: Each iteration moves `lo` right OR `hi` left — never both, never backward. So across all iterations, the total pointer movement is at most `n`. The while loop runs at most `n` times total, not `n` times per outer step.
📐 Pattern 2 — Same Direction (Fast + Slow)

Both pointers start at 0 and move right, but at different speeds or under different conditions. This pattern handles **subarrays with a property** (e.g., all elements ≤ k, no duplicates, sum ≤ limit) and **in-place array modification**.

sections.title: Longest Subarray with Sum ≤ K — O(n)

sections.label: Sliding Window = Two Pointers with a Window Size Twist
sections.content: When the window has a **fixed size** k, increment both `lo` and `hi` together each step. When the window has a **variable size** (longest/shortest satisfying a condition), use the shrink-while-invalid pattern shown above. Both are O(n) because each element enters and leaves the window at most once.
📐 Pattern 3 — Two Arrays (Merge / Count)
When comparing or merging two **separate sorted arrays**, use one pointer per array. Classic uses: merge step of merge sort, counting pairs across two arrays, intersection/union of sets.

sections.title: Count Pairs (a[i] + b[j] == target) Across Two Sorted Arrays

sections.content: 📐 Pattern 4 — In-Place Compaction
A slow pointer `w` (write head) and a fast pointer `r` (read head) let you remove/filter elements **in-place** without extra memory. The classic example is removing duplicates from a sorted array.

sections.title: Remove Duplicates In-Place from Sorted Array

sections.content: 📐 Pattern 5 — Three Pointers (3Sum)
Fix one element with an outer loop, then run opposite-ends two pointers on the remaining sorted range. This reduces the O(n³) brute force to **O(n²)** — the optimal for 3Sum-class problems.

sections.title: 3Sum — Count Triplets Summing to Zero

sections.content: 📊 Pattern Comparison Table

rows: r always advances; w advances on write

sections.label: 🚨 Contest Killer: Applying Two Pointers on Unsorted Data
sections.content: Two-pointer correctness usually **requires the array to be sorted** (or at least monotone). If the problem doesn't guarantee sorted order, sort it first (O(n log n)) — the O(n) two-pointer pass still dominates. Forgetting to sort is the single most common wrong answer on two-pointer problems.
⚡ Classic Codeforces Patterns

sections.title: Minimum Window Size with At Least K Distinct Values
Two-Pointer on Strings — Valid Palindrome After One Deletion

sections.content: 🏋️ Interactive Exercises

sections.title: Exercise 1: Trace the Opposite-Ends Pointer Movement
sections.explanation: Step 1: lo=0 (val 1), hi=4 (val 9). sum=10 == target. Print '1+9', break. steps becomes 1 before the break. Output: '1+9' then 'steps=1'.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Trace a Shrinking Sliding Window
sections.explanation: We want shortest subarray with sum >= 7. hi=0: sum=2. hi=1: sum=3. hi=2: sum=8>=7 → ans=min(INF,3)=3, remove a[0]=2, sum=6, lo=1. hi=3: sum=8>=7 → ans=min(3,3)=3, remove a[1]=1, sum=7>=7 → ans=min(3,2)=2, remove a[2]=5, sum=2, lo=3. hi=4: sum=5. Final ans=2. The subarray a[2..3]={5,2} has sum 7.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Count Pairs with Absolute Difference ≤ K
sections.approach: Since the array is sorted, arr[j] - arr[i] >= 0 for j > i. The condition becomes arr[j] - arr[i] <= k. For each j (right pointer), find the leftmost i such that arr[j] - arr[i] <= k using a left pointer that only moves forward. All indices from that leftmost i to j-1 form valid pairs with j. Sum these counts.
sections.explanation: For each hi, we maintain the smallest lo such that arr[hi]-arr[lo] <= k. Since the array is sorted, once arr[hi]-arr[lo] > k, arr[hi]-arr[lo-1] > k too, so lo never needs to go backward. The number of valid pairs ending at hi is exactly (hi - lo). Time: O(n) — lo moves right at most n times total. Space: O(1). For arr=[1,2,4,7], k=3: hi=1: lo=0, cnt+=1=1. hi=2: arr[2]-arr[0]=3<=3, cnt+=2=3. hi=3: arr[3]-arr[0]=6>3→lo=1; arr[3]-arr[1]=5>3→lo=2; arr[3]-arr[2]=3<=3, cnt+=1=4. Answer: 4. ✓

sections.title: Exercise 4: Minimum Size Subarray Sum ≥ S
sections.approach: Use a variable-size sliding window. Expand hi each step, adding arr[hi] to the window sum. Once sum >= s, record the window size and try to shrink from the left (advance lo) to find an even shorter valid window. Elements are positive, so shrinking from left always decreases the sum — making the monotone condition hold.
sections.explanation: Each element enters the window once (hi advances once) and leaves at most once (lo advances at most once). Total operations: O(2n) = O(n). The key insight is that since all values are positive, sum is monotonically increasing as hi grows and monotonically decreasing as lo grows — so the while(sum>=s) inner shrink will never 'miss' an optimal window. For [2,3,1,2,4,3], s=7: the minimum window is [4,3] of length 2. Space: O(1).

sections.title: Exercise 5: Max Water Container (Codeforces-style Height Array)
sections.approach: Start lo=0, hi=n-1. The area is min(h[lo],h[hi])*(hi-lo). To potentially improve, we must increase the minimum height — so move the pointer pointing to the SHORTER line inward. Moving the taller pointer would only decrease width without helping the minimum. This greedy choice is provably optimal.
sections.explanation: Correctness: suppose h[lo] < h[hi]. Moving hi inward gives width (hi-lo-1) and at most h[lo] height (since the left is the bottleneck), so water <= h[lo]*(hi-lo-1) < h[lo]*(hi-lo). Hence no valid pair with left=lo and right<hi can beat the current lo+hi pair if lo is the bottleneck — so we safely discard them all and advance lo. Time: O(n). Space: O(1). For h=[1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7], answer=49 (heights 8 and 7 with width 7).

sections.content: 📋 Quick Reference Summary

rows: One pointer per array, opposite dirs
